name: comfy
description: Opinionated, batteries-included 2D game engine built on wgpu, designed for fast iteration

kind.comment: Self-describes as a 2D game engine, but it is a code-first, batteries-included game loop (rendering/input/audio via draw_* / play_sound functions) with no editor or scene model — matches RESEARCH.md's 'framework' classification. Explicitly non-modular and opinionated.

license.comment: Dual-licensed MIT OR Apache-2.0 per crate metadata and the README License section. (GitHub's license detector surfaces only Apache-2.0.)

documentation.comment: No dedicated docs/book; the README states there is no documentation and points users to the examples/ folder. docs.rs hosts the API docs; project site is https://comfyengine.org/.

first-release.comment: GitHub repo created Aug 12, 2023; first real crates.io release (0.1.0) Sep 24, 2023.

latest-stable-version.comment: 0.4.0 published May 3, 2024 — the newest and max stable version on crates.io (checked 2026-07-06). No release since.

maintenance-status.comment: GitHub repo is flagged archived. README (Oct 24, 2025 update) states comfy is archived; the author left Rust gamedev and moved to C++/his own toolchain. No release since 0.4.0 (May 2024).

maturity.comment: Reached only 0.4; README warns the API is not stable and breaking changes will happen, and says 'this doesn't mean the engine is mature'. It did ship a commercial title (Unrelaxing Quacks). Now archived — no path to further stabilization. Scored beta rather than experimental because it was production-usable for small 2D games.

backing.value: Solo developer — Jakub Arnold (darthdeus) / LogLog Games; JetBrains provided OSS licenses.
backing.comment: Single-maintainer side project built alongside LogLog Games' own titles; no company or foundation backing. Now discontinued by the author.

implementation.comment: Pure Rust: wgpu renderer, winit windowing, kira audio, egui UI, hecs ECS — no non-Rust engine wrapped and no *-sys bindings in the core dependency set.

architecture.comment: Immediate-mode by design ('Simple immediate mode APIs for almost everything'): you call draw_circle/draw_sprite each frame and comfy batches + z-sorts. It embeds hecs and offers ECS helpers (ecs_sprite example) but an ECS is explicitly a non-goal and not required, so the dominant model is immediate.

built-in-ecs.comment: Embeds hecs and provides some helpers, but the README lists 'ECS based engine' as a non-goal and states it is 'by no means required or even optimal for most cases'. Not the primary data model, so false per RESEARCH.md.

dimensionality.comment: Explicitly a 2D engine; 3D is a stated non-goal (AAA 3D not supported, only simple 3D 'possible in the future'). No first-party 3D tooling, so 2D only.

scripting.comment: Gameplay is written in Rust; no embedded scripting language. (Low-level APIs are kept FFI-friendly for external callers, but that is not an in-engine scripting layer.)

data-driven-scenes.comment: No native scene/prefab format — the game is built in code. There is an LDtk example that loads level data from that third-party map editor's files, but comfy has no first-party serialized scene system.

reflection-serialization.comment: No engine-provided runtime reflection or game-state (de)serialization system; users can add serde themselves but comfy does not offer it first-party.

hot-reload.comment: Scope is shaders only: running with `--features dev` gives hot-reloading of user fragment shaders with in-game error display (added around v0.3). No Rust code or general asset hot reload.

visual-editor.comment: Code-first framework; ships no graphical scene/level editor.

live-inspector.comment: egui is bundled so you can build debug/inspector UIs yourself, but there is no first-party or de-facto runtime world/entity inspector.

asset-pipeline.comment: 'Parallel asset loading with support for most image and audio formats' plus optional include_dir asset packing — async loading of common formats with reasonable ergonomics (rating 3). No import/reprocessing pipeline or asset hot reload, so short of 5.

cli-project-tooling.comment: No dedicated CLI, cargo plugin, or official starter templates — a plain cargo project depending on the comfy crate.

debug-profiling-tools.comment: egui (and egui_plot) are bundled in the core, giving in-game debug overlays/plots. No integrated external profiler (no Tracy dependency).

ide-support.comment: Nothing beyond standard Rust tooling (rust-analyzer just works); no engine-specific IDE integration. (JetBrains sponsored the author with OSS licenses, which is unrelated to end-user IDE support.)

rendering-backend.comment: Built on wgpu (Vulkan/Metal/DX12/GL on native). On the web it targets WASM through WebGL 2 specifically (not WebGPU), which the README notes is required for its f16/HDR render textures.

pbr.comment: 2D engine; no PBR pipeline (3D/PBR is an explicit non-goal).

lighting.comment: Built-in 2D lighting with HDR, tonemapping and bloom. README notes the lights are basic and flagged for improvement on the (now-archived) roadmap.

shadows.comment: No shadow support; '2D shadowcasters with soft shadows' was listed as a roadmap item that never shipped.

custom-shaders.comment: User-defined fragment shaders with custom f32 uniforms landed in v0.3 (see fragment-shader.rs/.wgsl example). API is intentionally low-level/FFI-friendly.

sprites-tilemaps.comment: First-party automatic sprite batching and AnimatedSprite are core features. No native tilemap type, but an LDtk example demonstrates loading tile-based levels from that editor's format.

particle-system.comment: Built-in particles: simple API for individual particles plus configurable particle systems, and mesh-based trails.

post-processing.comment: HDR pipeline with bloom (hard-coded, always on in early versions) and tonemapping built in. Configurable bloom/post-processing was a roadmap item; chromatic-aberration is demonstrated via a custom shader example.

text-rendering.comment: Built-in text rendering (draw_text and friends), currently implemented via egui's painter on a separate layer.

physics.comment: No physics in the core crate. The official physics.rs example integrates 'blobs', a simple 2D physics engine by the same author, as a separate crate — closest to a first-party plugin/bring-your-own rather than bundled.

audio.comment: Audio ships in the core crate via a simple play_sound API, implemented on top of the kira crate.

input.comment: Keyboard and mouse via winit. No gamepad backend is present (no gilrs/gamepad dependency); 'Gamepad & touchpad support' was a roadmap item that never shipped. Touch is not first-party either.

animation.comment: Frame-based sprite animation via AnimatedSprite (animated_sprites example). No skeletal/tween/state-machine animation systems (2D-only, 3D/skeletal is a non-goal).

ui-system.comment: egui is bundled directly into comfy core (not an optional plugin) and is the built-in UI approach; the README expects it to remain the default 'until a better alternative comes along'.

networking.comment: No multiplayer/replication support, built-in or as an official plugin; bring your own.

linux.comment: Officially supported. winit is built with the x11 feature by default; Wayland is available behind an optional `wayland` cargo feature.

web-wasm.comment: Compiles to WASM and runs in the browser through WebGL 2. Author later expressed lost faith in wgpu/WebGPU for 2D web games, but WASM support was real and shipped.

ios.comment: Not listed among supported platforms (Windows/Linux/macOS/WASM); no documented iOS support.

android.comment: Not listed among supported platforms; no documented Android support.

clean-build-time.comment: Not measured on a consistent reference machine this pass; prefer null over a guess per RESEARCH.md. The README notes comfy is slower to compile than macroquad (it pulls the wgpu + egui + kira stack) but aims for 'reasonable' compile times.

incremental-build-time.comment: Not measured this pass; prefer null over a guess. comfy notably does not require end users to use proc macros, which helps rebuild times.

binary-size.comment: Not measured this pass; prefer null over a guess.

web-bundle-size.comment: Web-capable (WASM/WebGL2) but the WASM bundle was not measured on a reference build this pass; prefer null over a guess.

runtime-overhead.comment: Not measured with a defined methodology; prefer null over a guess.

github-stars.comment: Snapshot from the GitHub API on 2026-07-06 (repo is archived, so this is effectively final).

crates-io-downloads.comment: All-time downloads of the comfy crate as of 2026-07-06.

last-release.comment: 0.4.0 published May 3, 2024 on crates.io — the final release before archival.

shipped-titles.value: Unrelaxing Quacks (LogLog Games) — a fast 'survivors'-style game released on Steam.
shipped-titles.comment: Built by comfy's own author/studio. Also used for smaller jam entries (e.g. a 1-bit jam game). No third-party commercial titles widely documented.

community-size.comment: Effectively a single-maintainer project (Jakub Arnold); there is a Discord, but the README states few people ever used or saw the source, and the project is now archived.

plugin-asset-ecosystem.comment: Explicitly non-modular: 'There is no intention of having a plugin system.' No plugin/asset registry or third-party ecosystem.

learning-resources.comment: Many bundled examples (~40) plus a couple of announcement blog posts, but the README explicitly says there is no documentation and no aim for heavy docs coverage. No book/tutorial/video ecosystem. Scored 2.
